messages.content: You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is a single word. Count occurrences of each unique word and print 'WORD COUNT' one per line in input order of first appearance.

(set counts {})
(set order [])
(for-each w (split $0 "\n")
  (if (not (in w counts)) (push order w))
  (set counts (json_set counts [w] (add (get_or counts w 0) 1))))
(for-each k order
  (println (fmt "{} {}" k (json_get counts [k]))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is space-separated 'KEY VALUE' where VALUE is an integer. Sum VALUE by KEY and print 'KEY TOTAL' one per line in input order of first KEY appearance.

(set sums {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set parts (split line " "))
  (set k (first parts))
  (set v (parse_int (last parts)))
  (if (not (in k sums)) (push order k))
  (set sums (json_set sums [k] (add (get_or sums k 0) v))))
(for-each k order
  (println (fmt "{} {}" k (json_get sums [k]))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is a string. Find the line that appears most frequently. Print just that line (if tied, print the first one to reach the max).

(set counts {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (if (not (in line counts)) (push order line))
  (set counts (json_set counts [line] (add (get_or counts line 0) 1))))
(set best "")
(set best_count 0)
(for-each k order
  (if (gt (json_get counts [k]) best_count)
    (do (set best k) (set best_count (json_get counts [k])))))
(println best)

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is space-separated tokens. Count distinct tokens per line and print just the count for each line, one per line.

(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set seen {})
  (set count 0)
  (for-each tok (split line " ")
    (if (not (in tok seen))
      (do (set seen (json_set seen [tok] true)) (set count (add count 1)))))
  (println count))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is one word. Print the top 3 most common words by count, descending, formatted 'WORD: COUNT' one per line. If a tie occurs in count, break by input order of first appearance.

(set counts {})
(set order [])
(set idx 0)
(set first_idx {})
(for-each w (split $0 "\n")
  (if (not (in w counts))
    (do (push order w) (set first_idx (json_set first_idx [w] idx))))
  (set counts (json_set counts [w] (add (get_or counts w 0) 1)))
  (set idx (add idx 1)))
(set ranked (sort_by order (\k (sub 0 (mul (json_get counts [k]) 1000000)))))
(for-each k (slice ranked 0 3)
  (println (fmt "{}: {}" k (json_get counts [k]))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.

Each line of arg0 is comma-separated 'CATEGORY,AMOUNT' where AMOUNT is a decimal. Sum AMOUNT by CATEGORY and print 'CATEGORY: TOTAL' (TOTAL formatted with 2 decimals) one per line in input order of first appearance.

(set sums {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set parts (split line ","))
  (set k (first parts))
  (set v (float (last parts)))
  (if (not (in k sums)) (push order k))
  (set sums (json_set sums [k] (add (get_or sums k 0.0) v))))
(for-each k order
  (println (fmt "{}: {:.2f}" k (json_get sums [k]))))

Each line of arg0 is a tab-separated row 'IP\tSTATUS' where STATUS is an integer code. Count how many rows have each STATUS code. Print 'CODE COUNT' one per line in input order of first appearance.

(set counts {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set code (last (split line "\t")))
  (if (not (in code counts)) (push order code))
  (set counts (json_set counts [code] (add (get_or counts code 0) 1))))
(for-each k order
  (println (fmt "{} {}" k (json_get counts [k]))))

The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. Each line of arg0 is whitespace-separated 'TIMESTAMP LEVEL MESSAGE'. Count how many lines have each LEVEL. Print 'LEVEL COUNT' one per line in input order of first appearance.

(set counts {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set level (array_get (split line " ") 1))
  (if (not (in level counts)) (push order level))
  (set counts (json_set counts [level] (add (get_or counts level 0) 1))))
(for-each k order
  (println (fmt "{} {}" k (json_get counts [k]))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Each line of arg0 is space-separated 'KEY VALUE' integer pairs. Print the SINGLE key with the largest sum of values. (If tied, the first to reach the max.)

(set sums {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set parts (split line " "))
  (set k (first parts))
  (set v (parse_int (last parts)))
  (if (not (in k sums)) (push order k))
  (set sums (json_set sums [k] (add (get_or sums k 0) v))))
(set best "")
(set best_sum -999999)
(for-each k order
  (if (gt (json_get sums [k]) best_sum)
    (do (set best k) (set best_sum (json_get sums [k])))))
(println best)

Each line of arg0 is one word. Print the count of DISTINCT words as a single integer.

(set seen {})
(for-each w (split $0 "\n")
  (set seen (json_set seen [w] true)))
(println (len (map_keys seen)))

Each line of arg0 is space-separated tokens. Sum the count of tokens across all lines. Print as a single integer.
(println (sum (map_arr (split $0 "\n") (\line (len (split line " "))))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.

Each line of arg0 is comma-separated 'CATEGORY,VALUE' where VALUE is an integer. Print the top-2 categories with the largest sum of VALUE, descending, formatted 'CATEGORY: SUM' one per line. Tie-break by input order of first appearance.

(set sums {})
(set order [])
(for-each line (split $0 "\n")
  (set parts (split line ","))
  (set k (first parts))
  (set v (parse_int (last parts)))
  (if (not (in k sums)) (push order k))
  (set sums (json_set sums [k] (add (get_or sums k 0) v))))
(set ranked (sort_by order (\k (sub 0 (json_get sums [k])))))
(for-each k (slice ranked 0 2)
  (println (fmt "{}: {}" k (json_get sums [k]))))

You write Sigil programs. Sigil is an S-expression language designed for minimal tokens. CLI args: $0 is the first user arg as a string. The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. For aggregation tasks the canonical pattern is: build a map with (json_set m [key] (add (get_or m key 0) ...)) inside a (for-each), then iterate (map_keys m) to print results. Use short aliases: len, str, fmt, split, join, sort, push, filter, map_arr, reduce, sort_by, json_set, json_get, get_or, map_keys, in. Output ONLY raw Sigil code.
Convert the TSV input in arg0 (with header row) to a Markdown table. Use ' | ' as separator and pipe-padded edges, and add a '| --- | --- | ... |' divider row after the header.

(set rows (split $0 "\n"))
(set header_fields (split (first rows) "\t"))
(println (fmt "| {} |" (join header_fields " | ")))
(set dividers (map_arr header_fields (\f "---")))
(println (fmt "| {} |" (join dividers " | ")))
(for-each row (rest rows)
  (println (fmt "| {} |" (join (split row "\t") " | "))))

The harness passes ALL input as a single multi-line string in $0; use (split $0 "\n") to iterate lines. Convert the CSV input in arg0 (with header row) to a Markdown table. Use ' | ' as separator and pipe-padded edges, and add a '| --- | --- | ... |' divider row after the header.

(set rows (split $0 "\n"))
(set header_fields (split (first rows) ","))
(println (fmt "| {} |" (join header_fields " | ")))
(set dividers (map_arr header_fields (\f "---")))
(println (fmt "| {} |" (join dividers " | ")))
(for-each row (rest rows)
  (println (fmt "| {} |" (join (split row ",") " | "))))
